one_line_takeaway: A structured, taxonomy-driven survey of LLM attacks (10 types across training vs. inference) and 16 prevention/detection defenses, whose central deliverable is a coverage matrix showing that few defenses are robustly effective and that model inversion has no known countermeasure.
key_finding: Only a handful of defenses are rated High and, per the authors, 'High' means the defense can in principle be scaled to match attacker sophistication: retokenization (against adversarial input, jailbreaking, prompt injection), SmoothLLM (jailbreaking), fine-tuning/fine-mixing (backdoor), anomaly detection (data poisoning), differential privacy (membership inference), and dimensional masking (embedding inversion). Most other defenses (paraphrasing, delimiters/spotlighting, sandwich and instructional prevention, embedding purification, perplexity/naive-LLM/response-based detection, perturbation, MDP) are only Moderate and can be evaded by a skilled attacker. Two notable gaps are highlighted: differential privacy is Low against gradient leakage (noise insufficient vs. large-scale gradient correlations, and a privacy/utility trade-off), and model inversion attacks have NO known effective defense (marked N/A / 'without known defenses').
core_contribution: A survey (not an empirical study) by Aguilera-Martinez and Berzal (University of Granada) that defines and organizes the LLM security landscape into a single structured framework. Its own contribution is a two-part taxonomy: (1) attacks categorized by LLM life-cycle stage into training-time/causative attacks (backdoor, data poisoning, gradient leakage) versus test-time/exploratory attacks on deployed models (adversarial input, jailbreaking, prompt injection, model inversion, data extraction, membership inference, embedding inversion); and (2) defenses split into prevention-based and detection-based mechanisms. The synthesizing deliverable is a coverage matrix (Table 2) that maps each of 16 defenses to the attack types it addresses and assigns a qualitative protection effectiveness rating (Low/Moderate/High), plus a companion attack taxonomy table (Table 1) tagging each attack by whether it targets model integrity, data privacy, or both.
threat_model: The survey adopts a classic ML-security threat taxonomy (Barreno-style capability dimension): causative attackers who can influence the training data/process, and exploratory attackers who cannot alter the trained model but can query it and observe outputs. Threats span both model integrity (inducing incorrect/attacker-chosen outputs, e.g. backdoor triggers, poisoning, adversarial inputs, jailbreaking, prompt injection) and data privacy (reconstructing or inferring training data, e.g. gradient leakage, model/embedding inversion, data extraction, membership inference). The authors explicitly scope OUT model-stealing attacks that target trained parameters/hyperparameters. Attacker knowledge is characterized per-attack as black-box (query/oracle access only) vs white-box (access to parameters/gradients/architecture), and auxiliary-free vs auxiliary-based for gradient leakage.
limitations: As a survey it contributes no new measurements: effectiveness ratings are the authors' qualitative judgements, not benchmark numbers, and no head-to-head evaluation, dataset, or metric backs the Low/Moderate/High labels. Coverage is a snapshot ('at the time of this writing') and explicitly excludes model-stealing and broader agentic/tool-use attack surfaces. The formal definitions are borrowed from cited work rather than re-derived or validated. The matrix collapses heterogeneous attacks/defenses into a single three-level scale, so relative severity, cost, and attacker assumptions are not quantified. There is also minor internal reliance on parameter/architecture facts cited from vendors (e.g. the GPT-4 mixture-of-experts description) that are not independently verified.

quantitative_results: Attack taxonomy (Table 1): 3 training-time attacks (backdoor, data poisoning, gradient leakage) and 7 attacks on trained models (adversarial input, jailbreaking, prompt injection, model inversion, data extraction, membership inference, embedding inversion) = 10 attack types total.

Defense taxonomy: 16 defenses = 10 prevention-based (paraphrasing, retokenization, delimiters, sandwich prevention, instructional prevention, embedding purification, SmoothLLM, dimensional masking, differential privacy, fine-tuning) + 6 detection-based (perplexity-based, naive LLM-based, response-based, perturbation, masking-differential prompting, anomaly detection).

Table 2 effectiveness ratings (authors' qualitative judgement): retokenization = High vs adversarial input / jailbreaking / prompt injection; SmoothLLM = High vs jailbreaking; dimensional masking = High vs embedding inversion; fine-tuning = High vs backdoor; anomaly detection = High vs data poisoning; differential privacy = Low vs gradient leakage but High vs membership inference; model inversion = no known defense (N/A).

Background parameter counts cited from prior work (NOT the paper's own results): GPT-3.5 ~175B params; GPT-4 described as MoE of 8 x 220B; Llama 2 7B-70B; Llama 3 largest 405B with 128K-token context; Grok-1 314B with 8,192-token context.
